petitioner_background: The petitioner is a high school special education teacher seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in education under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. She has worked as a special education teacher at Redemptorist High School in Baton Rouge, Louisiana since 2004, and previously taught at La Salle Green Hills Grade School in Mandaluyong City, Philippines from 1980 to 2004. The petition was filed on June 29, 2007.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a certificate of appreciation from the Cordell Hull Foundation for International Education acknowledging her 'contribution as a J-1 Exchange Visitor Teacher to the Catholic Schools of Baton Rouge' during the 2004-2005 school year; and (2) July 2007 letters from the Filipino Coordinator and the Registrar of the La Salle Green Hills Grade School stating she was nominated for its 'Model Teacher Award' and 'St. Benilde Teacher Exemplar Award.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found no evidence showing the Cordell Hull Foundation certificate is a nationally or internationally recognized prize or award for excellence in education. Regarding the La Salle Green Hills awards, there was no evidence that the petitioner actually received either award — she was only nominated. The AAO emphasized that the plain language of the criterion requires receipt of nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards, and that these awards reflect local or institutional recognition rather than national or international recognition.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted an April 22, 2004 issue of the NCTM Daily, an on-site newspaper for the National Council of Teachers of Mathematics Annual Meeting. The petitioner's photograph appeared in the 'Question of the Day' section alongside photographs of seven other teachers, each providing a one-sentence response to the question 'What Would Be Most Helpful in Making You a Better Teacher?' The petitioner responded: 'I would like to know the techniques and how to make the teaching more lively.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that a captioned photograph of the petitioner does not meet the plain language of the criterion, which requires published material to be 'about the alien.' The material was not primarily about the petitioner and did not identify an author. Further, there was no evidence showing that this conference newspaper, distributed during the four days of the NCTM Annual Meeting, qualifies as a professional or major trade publication or other form of major media. The AAO noted that to qualify as major media, a publication should have significant national or international distribution.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted several letters of support discussing her talent as a teacher and her activities in the field. She also submitted evidence that she coauthored elementary school textbooks.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the letters of support included no substantive discussion as to which specific educational achievements constitute original contributions of major significance in the field. The AAO emphasized that contributions must be not only original but of major significance, and that 'major significance' is not superfluous. While the petitioner coauthored elementary school textbooks, there was no evidence demonstrating her work had major significance — the record did not indicate the extent of her influence on other teachers nationally or internationally, nor show that the field had changed as a result of her work. The AAO noted that letters solicited by an alien in support of an immigration petition are of less weight than preexisting, independent evidence of original contributions of major significance, citing Matter of Caron International. Without extensive documentation showing her work has been unusually influential or highly acclaimed, the AAO could not conclude she met this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence showing that she coauthored educational textbooks in the Philippines that were published and distributed nationally. On appeal, the petitioner submitted evidence showing that her work has been cited.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the petitioner established she meets this single criterion. The AAO noted that the regulatory criteria are separate and distinct from one another, and because separate criteria exist for authorship of scholarly articles and original contributions of major significance, CIS does not view the two as interchangeable.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted her contract with the Diocese of Baton Rouge for the 2007-2008 school year reflecting that she commanded a salary of $30,040.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that the plain language of the criterion requires evidence of a high salary 'in relation to others in the field.' The petitioner offered no basis for comparison showing that her salary in 2007 or any other prior year was significantly high in relation to others in her field.

outcome_reasoning: The petitioner established that she meets only one of the regulatory criteria (scholarly articles), while three are required to establish eligibility. The AAO found the petitioner failed to demonstrate receipt of a major, internationally recognized award, or satisfaction of at least three criteria. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished herself to such an extent as to have achieved sustained national or international acclaim or to be within the small percentage at the very top of her field. The evidence was not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field at the national or international level. The burden of proof remained with the petitioner, who did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791, 795 (Comm'r 1988)
